overview: US warplanes struck Iran for a thirteenth straight night as President Trump — now moving B-1 bombers toward the region — said he remains close to deciding on a 'massive attack' larger than the campaign so far, widely read as the buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' nuclear complex. Trump opened a new coercive front, vowing to make Iran pay war 'damages' out of its own frozen assets, which Tehran called 'incendiary,' while Iran's Revolutionary Guard said it stopped three tankers in the Strait of Hormuz and branded UK bases used by American bombers 'legitimate targets' — prompting London to say it is 'ready to defend itself.' At home, the House voted 214-208 to end the war, but Senate Republicans voted to let Trump keep waging it.

keyPoints.text: Trump said he is still 'close' to deciding on a 'massive attack' on Iran larger than any yet, and the US deployed a B-1 bomber toward the region as the air campaign intensified.
Trump vowed to fund Iran-war 'damages' from frozen Iranian assets the US 'controls'; Tehran called the threat 'incendiary,' and Iran's Araghchi said 'mindless aggression' won't yield a deal.
CENTCOM said it completed a thirteenth consecutive night of strikes; Iran's Revolutionary Guard said it stopped three oil tankers from passing through the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran's IRGC declared UK bases used by US bombers 'legitimate targets,' naming a site in south-west England; Britain said it is 'ready to defend itself.'
The House passed a war-powers resolution 214-208 to end the war — its second rebuke — but Senate Republicans voted to let Trump keep waging it, leaving no binding brake.
Brent crude held above $100 a barrel and shares stayed under pressure, with Hormuz war-risk insurance running roughly four times its five-year average as a second sea route turned kinetic.
The US toll stands at a contested 17–18 dead and nearly 100 wounded; the NYT reported the count shown on a Pentagon website appears lower than tallies elsewhere.

watch.text: Whether Trump orders the 'massive attack' he says he is close to deciding on — widely read as the deeply buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' nuclear complex, and now shadowed by a B-1 bomber deployment — the single largest rung of escalation on the table, and how Iran answers it.

Whether Iran acts on its threat to treat UK (and other allied) bases hosting US strike aircraft as 'legitimate targets,' widening the war onto a NATO member's territory, and whether the frozen-assets 'damages' threat draws an Iranian or legal response.

Whether either chamber can align on a binding war-powers measure after the House voted 214-208 to end the war and Senate Republicans voted to let it continue, and whether the Senate takes up the ~$95bn war budget and $1.15tn military bill.

stories.title: Trump still 'close' to a 'massive attack' as a B-1 bomber deploys and strikes hit a thirteenth night
stories.summary: President Trump said he remains close to deciding whether to launch 'a massive attack' on Iran — on a scale larger than the strikes already waged over nearly two weeks — the Guardian and Axios reported, as the US visibly hardened its posture: Axios reported a B-1 bomber was deployed toward the Middle East 'as attacks on Iran intensify.' The threat, widely read as pointing at the deeply buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' (Kuh-e Kolang) nuclear complex near Natanz that Trump earlier vowed to hit 'very heavily,' keeps the war's nuclear dimension open with IAEA safeguards suspended and no inspectors inside. Iran's foreign minister Abbas Araghchi — Tehran's top diplomat, who survived the day-one strike that killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei — said 'mindless aggression' would not help produce a deal, as UN Secretary-General António Guterres's warning that the Middle East is 'getting out of control' hung over a Security Council still without a ceasefire.
stories.explainer: A 'massive attack' larger than the campaign so far most plausibly means the hardened, roughly 100-metre-deep Pickaxe Mountain site — a different order of escalation from bombing shipping or power plants. The B-1 deployment matters because it is the physical scaffolding a strike on a buried target would need: a long-range heavy bomber able to carry the largest conventional bunker-busting ordnance. Moving one toward the region converts a rhetorical threat into a visible capability, narrowing the gap between Trump's words and an order — and raising the stakes of either a failed strike on hardened rock or an uncontrolled release near nuclear material that locked-out inspectors could not independently assess.

stories.title: Trump vows to make Iran pay war 'damages' from its own frozen assets; Tehran calls it 'incendiary'
stories.summary: President Trump said the damages from the war would be 'paid for by Iranian money' that the United States 'has in its possession, and controls' — opening a financial front alongside the military one, Al Jazeera and the BBC reported. Iran's foreign ministry slammed the threat as 'incendiary,' and foreign minister Abbas Araghchi said 'mindless aggression' would not advance any deal. The remarks refer to Iranian assets frozen under US and international sanctions, which Trump is proposing to convert into a form of war reparations while American bombs still fall — a move Tehran casts as theft and a fresh obstacle to the third-party diplomacy Iran says continues through intermediaries. It lands as the war's cost mounts: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth put the US bill at $37.5bn and asked Congress for nearly $88bn more.
stories.explainer: Frozen assets are Iranian funds — central-bank reserves and oil revenues — parked in foreign accounts and blocked by sanctions, which Tehran cannot access but the US and its partners can restrict. Proposing to spend them on war 'damages' is legally fraught and politically explosive: it would treat seized sovereign money as a reparations pool before any settlement, hardening Iran's sense that negotiation is pointless because Washington will simply take what it wants. Coming while strikes continue, it also links the war's soaring cost at home — the $37.5bn bill and the fight in Congress over funding it — to a promise that Iran, not the US taxpayer, will foot it, an argument aimed as much at Trump's own restless base as at Tehran.

stories.title: CENTCOM completes a thirteenth night of strikes as Iran says it stopped three tankers in Hormuz
stories.summary: US Central Command said it completed its latest strikes on Iran, extending the campaign to a thirteenth consecutive night, the US Department of War and Al Jazeera reported, with the military again framing the operation as degrading Tehran's ability to threaten shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran answered on the water: the Guardian reported that Iran's Revolutionary Guard said it had stopped three oil tankers from passing through the strait, tightening its declared closure of the chokepoint just as the Houthis pressed a second front in the Red Sea. The unbroken run of strikes — now stretching close to two weeks since the interim ceasefire collapsed on 8 July — has continued through congressional votes on the war's cost and its future, mutual threats against civilian infrastructure, and stalled third-party diplomacy, underlining that the fighting has settled into a grinding tempo neither the backlash nor the talks has interrupted.
stories.explainer: A thirteenth consecutive night is less a single event than evidence of a war that has become routine: the strikes no longer spike or pause around diplomatic openings or votes, they simply continue. Iran's claim to have stopped three tankers is the mirror image — its way of showing the closure of Hormuz is not merely declared but enforced, ship by ship. The two together capture the deadlock over the strait: the US bombs nightly to force it open, Iran halts tankers to keep it shut, and neither has changed the other's behaviour, which is why the risk premium on every Gulf voyage keeps climbing even without a decisive blow.

stories.title: Iran names UK bases 'legitimate targets'; Britain says it is 'ready to defend itself'
stories.summary: Iran's Revolutionary Guard warned that any bases being used by the United States to launch strikes on Iran are 'legitimate targets,' and specifically claimed that a base in south-west England had been used by US forces to fly bombing missions, the Guardian reported. The British government responded that the UK is 'ready to defend itself,' acknowledging that it has allowed the US to launch operations from its territory. The threat widens the war's declared target set beyond the Gulf and Iran's immediate neighbours to the homeland of a nuclear-armed NATO member, following Iran's pattern of retaliating against the states that host and enable the American campaign — from Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase, where two US troops were killed, to claimed strikes on Gulf allies. No attack on UK soil has been reported; the exchange is, for now, a rhetorical escalation over where the war might spread.
stories.explainer: Threatening a base in Britain is a sharp geographic escalation because it moves the war's logic — hit whoever enables the strikes — onto the territory of a major Western power thousands of miles from the Gulf. It is almost certainly aimed more at pressure than at action: Iran lacks the reach to strike England conventionally, so the value is in forcing London to weigh the cost of hosting US operations and in signalling that no ally is beyond the war's rhetorical frontier. But it hardens the coalition rather than splitting it — Britain's 'ready to defend itself' is the response of a government digging in, not backing out — and it raises the risk that a single miscalculation could draw a NATO member directly into the fighting.

stories.title: House votes 214-208 to end the war; Senate Republicans vote to let Trump keep waging it
stories.summary: The US House of Representatives passed a war-powers resolution directing President Trump to end the Iran conflict by a vote of 214-208, with four Republicans crossing the aisle — the second time the chamber has voted to rein in the war, NBC, NPR, the Guardian and Al Jazeera reported. But Congress delivered no binding brake: the Senate took up a rival measure and its Republican majority voted to let Trump keep waging the war, so no joint resolution reached his desk, and any that did would face a veto. The split lands roughly a day after the same House voted to fund the campaign with a ~$95bn budget and a $1.15tn military bill, putting Washington on record bankrolling the war, ordering it stopped, and declining to force the issue all in one week. Senator Rand Paul, a Kentucky Republican long opposed to the war, said the US is 'worse off than we were before.'
stories.explainer: A war-powers resolution is Congress's constitutional tool to force a president out of hostilities he never obtained a declaration of war for — but it binds only if both chambers pass it and it survives a veto, which needs a two-thirds override. The 214-208 House vote, carried with four Republican defections, is a real majority against the war; the Senate Republicans' vote to let it continue is what strips that majority of legal force. The result is a loud, divided political signal and no off-switch: the House is on record against the war, the Senate keeps the president's hand free, and the rift between a furious legislature and the White House prosecuting the campaign widens without changing what the military does tonight.

stories.title: Oil holds above $100 as two chokepoints stay kinetic
stories.summary: Brent crude, which broke above $100 a barrel for the first time in two months as the Houthi attacks on Saudi tankers and Trump's 'massive attack' talk stacked fresh risk onto the market, held near that level as the war pressed on two oil arteries at once, the Guardian, CNBC and NBC reported. Analysts tied the surge to fears the Houthi blockade could strangle Saudi exports — the supply the market had counted on when Hormuz tightened — and Iran's claim to have stopped three tankers in the strait only sharpened the squeeze. War-risk insurance for tankers transiting the Strait of Hormuz has climbed to roughly four times its five-year average, Al Jazeera reported, a cost that flows into freight rates and, in time, pump prices; US average gasoline remains above $4 a gallon.
stories.explainer: Crude above $100 is what it looks like when a market stops assuming some oil always gets through. With Hormuz a shooting zone and the Red Sea now contested, traders can no longer price Saudi Arabia as the reliable relief valve, so the risk premium stays wide even on days without a decisive blow. Insurance is the transmission mechanism: quadrupled war-risk premiums make every voyage costlier whether or not a given tanker is hit, and those costs compound through freight and refining into the price of fuel worldwide — the channel by which a war over two straits reaches households far from the Gulf.

stories.title: US death toll stays contested as a Pentagon website appears to show fewer
stories.summary: The number of US service members the war has killed remained contested and politically charged, with the New York Times reporting that the death toll displayed on a Pentagon website appears lower than tallies compiled elsewhere. Independent counts have put the figure at 17 to 18: an earlier ABC News/Time tally reached 17, while the Guardian, profiling four of the dead — Angel Rampersad, Michael Swinton, Isabella Gonzales and Tyler Feehan — described them as among '18 US troops killed in the war.' The Pentagon has acknowledged nearly 100 wounded, a day after the Times earlier reported dozens of injuries had been withheld. The discrepancies land after President Trump attended the dignified transfer of the fallen even as ABC News reported he appeared to downplay the deaths online.
stories.explainer: The dead and wounded are the war's most politically potent variable at home: each named casualty turns an abstract tally into a concrete cost, feeding the congressional fights over funding and war powers. That an official Pentagon page shows a lower number than journalists' counts sharpens the suspicion — already raised when injuries were withheld — that the administration is managing the toll's optics as much as reporting it. The direction is clear even if the exact figure is not: a count creeping from 17 toward 18 across several countries, in a war whose losses the president both honours in person and minimises online.

stories.title: Trump is 'losing patience' over a war with no clear end, as his base sours
stories.summary: The Wall Street Journal reported that President Trump is losing patience over an Iran war with no clear end in sight, capturing a president boxed in by a campaign he cannot quickly win or cleanly exit. The frustration compounds a new poll — described by Politico as leaving Trump in 'a politically impossible situation' — showing even his own voters souring on the war, which The Economist has called the least popular US war since modern polling began, its support below the low points of the Iraq and Vietnam eras. The erosion within the president's base underpins the House's move to end the war, the hostile Senate reception Hegseth met over the war's cost, and the very talk of a decisive 'massive attack' as a way to force an outcome.
stories.explainer: Impatience at the top is itself a strategic variable, because it pushes toward the two opposite exits at once: a negotiated wind-down, or a single overwhelming blow meant to 'finish' the war before the politics turn further. A president fighting a war his base rejects has less room to grind on but strong incentive not to be seen abandoning it after 17–18 deaths — which is precisely the bind that makes a high-risk 'massive attack' more tempting even as Congress and the public pull the other way.

stories.title: Houthis hit Saudi tankers and press a second 'Gate of Tears' front in the Red Sea
stories.summary: Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthi movement attacked a Saudi oil tanker in the Red Sea and claimed to have struck another vessel, the BBC, PBS and Al Jazeera reported — following through on the 'maritime embargo' it declared on Saudi Arabia and turning its threatened second front kinetic. The group has warned it will make Bab al-Mandeb — the strait at the Red Sea's southern mouth whose name translates as the 'Gate of Tears' — a live chokepoint for any tanker calling at Saudi ports, layering a second blockade atop Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The strikes rippled straight into markets, helping push oil above $100, and stoked what the BBC called fears of a wider Middle East conflict and deeper global economic damage as the war presses on two oil arteries at once.
stories.explainer: Iran fights asymmetrically, and the Houthis let it open a second maritime front it need not staff itself: where Iran squeezes Hormuz, its Yemeni ally squeezes Bab al-Mandeb and the Red Sea route to Saudi ports. A blockade only reshapes markets once it is enforced — insurers and shipping lines price a declaration as risk but reroute for real once tankers are actually hit. Striking Saudi crude is that threshold, because Saudi Arabia was the one large Gulf producer the market treated as a relief valve when Hormuz tightened; hitting its exports shrinks the cushion that has so far kept the war from becoming a full global oil crisis.

stories.title: US signs a nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia mid-war
stories.summary: The United States signed a civil-nuclear cooperation deal with Saudi Arabia, the Guardian reported, deepening ties with Riyadh in the middle of the Iran war. The timing is striking: Washington is extending nuclear cooperation to Saudi Arabia even as US bombs fall on Iran's nuclear facilities and threaten the buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' complex, and as Iran's Houthi allies attack Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea. As the Guardian's Kenneth Roth noted, the agreement offers the Saudis 'the same options Trump has sworn Iran will never have' — a contrast sharpened by Riyadh's past signals that it would want enrichment capacity, or its own program, if Iran retained one. Coming now, the deal reads as Washington binding a key Gulf partner closer as the war reorders the region's alignments.
stories.explainer: A civil-nuclear deal is about reactors and fuel, not weapons, but with Saudi Arabia the line is politically fraught: Riyadh has said it would match Iran's nuclear capabilities, so the terms — especially whether the US permits uranium enrichment on Saudi soil — determine whether the deal caps proliferation or nudges it. Signing it while striking Iran's nuclear sites signals that Washington is trying to lock in a compliant Gulf nuclear order on its own terms, and it further entangles Saudi Arabia in a war in which its tankers are already being hit.

stories.title: Iran warns of an 'eye for an eye' if the US destroys its infrastructure
stories.summary: Iran warned it would respond 'eye for an eye' if the US follows through on President Trump's threat to destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant for every Iranian attack on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, the Guardian reported. The warning hardens Trump's per-attack retaliation formula into a mutual one: each side now publicly ties a specific response to the other's action. Iran has already said it would answer such attacks by sparking power outages in the Gulf states that host US troops. The exchange keeps the war's escalation on autopilot — a ladder that climbs a rung with every ship struck and every span or plant hit — with neither leader left much room to step back without appearing to concede.
stories.explainer: 'Eye for an eye' is the same trap as Trump's per-attack formula, seen from the other side: once both leaders have publicly committed to automatic retaliation, following through is default and backing down looks like weakness, so the fighting escalates without either side deciding to escalate. Because Iran's chosen response — blacking out the Gulf states hosting US bases — punishes third parties, it turns those host governments into pressure points, giving them every incentive to lean on Washington for a ceasefire before the exchange reaches them.

stories.title: 'Pickaxe Mountain' is the likely target of the threatened 'massive attack'
stories.summary: President Trump's talk of a 'massive attack' on Iran has refocused attention on the complex known in the West as 'Pickaxe Mountain' (Kuh-e Kolang), sited near the Natanz enrichment plant and estimated to be buried almost 100 metres inside a mountain, the Guardian reported — far harder to destroy than surface sites already hit, and little understood publicly. Trump had already vowed to attack the underground facility 'very heavily.' The threat lands with IAEA safeguards access suspended since earlier strikes on Natanz and Iran having called off its interim-deal commitments, so no inspectors are inside to gauge the consequences of any strike — a gap sharpened by explosions reported near the Bushehr nuclear plant during recent nights, and now by a B-1 bomber deployment toward the region.
stories.explainer: Striking a hardened, deeply buried nuclear site is a different order of escalation from bombing shipping or power plants. A facility 100 metres underground is built to survive conventional bombs, so hitting it 'very heavily' implies the largest bunker-busting ordnance — the kind a heavy bomber like the B-1 exists to carry — and raises the prospect of either failure or an uncontrolled release near sensitive nuclear material. With inspectors locked out, no one — neither side nor the outside world — would have an independent reading of what a strike actually did.

stories.title: Strikes and mutual threats keep overshadowing ceasefire talks
stories.summary: US-mediated efforts to salvage the collapsed interim ceasefire, which had moved to Pakistan, remain overshadowed as a thirteenth consecutive night of strikes, Secretary of State Marco Rubio's judgment that Iran is 'not serious about a deal,' and Trump's threat to seize Iran's frozen assets undercut the diplomacy, AP and the Guardian reported. Mediators had pressed a specific proposal — earlier framed as a 10-day ceasefire — to pause the fighting long enough for both sides to step back from the chokepoints, but the effort runs against a hardening war: Hegseth would set no timeline, Iran has called off its interim-deal commitments and Araghchi says 'mindless aggression' won't help, while Trump weighs a 'massive attack.' Iran says diplomatic contacts continue through intermediaries, leaving negotiation the war's thinnest — and still only — off-ramp.
stories.explainer: With the interim deal dead and IAEA inspectors locked out, there is no standing framework to fall back on, so any de-escalation must be rebuilt from scratch through third parties. Talks that proceed under a thirteenth night of bombing, mutual per-attack retaliation formulas, a threat to convert Iran's frozen money into war reparations, and a public US verdict that Tehran is not serious have little leverage: each strike and each hardening statement raises the price of being seen to concede.

stories.title: Iran's reprisals keep falling on US allies that host the campaign
stories.summary: Iran's retaliation has continued to fall on the US-aligned states that ring it — and now, rhetorically, on their distant backers — rather than on American soil directly, with recent reprisals against Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan, whose army said it intercepted four of six Iranian missiles fired earlier in the week. Iran has separately claimed it struck Amazon infrastructure in Bahrain and set a Kuwaiti electricity-and-water plant ablaze, matching its warning that if US strikes persist 'no political border' will be safe — a threat it extended this week to UK bases used by American bombers. A leaked US assessment concluded Iran retained roughly 70% of its ballistic missiles after the spring campaign.
stories.explainer: Hitting the US through the states that shelter and enable its bases raises the cost of the American campaign while pressuring those governments to lean on Washington for a ceasefire. Widening the declared target set from Gulf bases and power plants to cloud infrastructure and now a base in Britain is a way to make hosting American forces politically and economically unbearable — even where Iran cannot actually reach with missiles, the threat alone forces allies to weigh the price of enabling the war.

stories.title: The war's $37.5bn bill and $88bn ask, still before the Senate
stories.summary: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate the US-Israel war on Iran has cost $37.5bn so far and asked for nearly $88bn more — including roughly $67bn to restock depleted munitions — the Guardian, PBS and Reuters reported, drawing open bipartisan hostility, with Michigan Democrat Gary Peters telling him 'you, sir, are the failure' and Hegseth refusing to give any timeline for the war's end. The House answered by passing a roughly $95bn war budget and a $1.15tn military bill; the Senate, where the hearing turned hostile, has not taken up the money, and Congress remains split over whether to fund the war or force its end — the same chamber whose Republicans this week voted to let Trump keep waging it.
stories.explainer: Once the Pentagon must return to Congress mid-war for tens of billions more, the legislature acquires a financial choke-point it otherwise lacks. The split between a House that funded the war and a Senate that grilled Hegseth over it is the central domestic fault line — and Hegseth's refusal to name an end date is exactly what hardens senators' resolve to attach conditions, and what powers the war-powers push even as the Senate GOP declines to force the issue. The $67bn to restock arms is the tell that the US has been firing munitions faster than it can replace them, a material limit on how long the air campaign can last.

stories.title: BBC returns to the Minab school where an opening-day strike killed scores of children
stories.summary: Nearly five months after the war began, the BBC visited the girls' school in Minab, southern Iran, where a strike on the conflict's opening day killed scores of children — the BBC's account put the toll at around 120 — speaking to a teacher and a mother still living with the horror. The report is a rare on-the-ground reckoning with the human cost of the campaign that opened on 28 February with the largest Israeli combat sortie in history, and a reminder that beneath the shifting fronts and funding votes the war's toll on Iranian civilians has been accumulating since its first hours.
stories.explainer: Casualty figures from the war's chaotic opening remain contested — early tallies of the Minab strike ranged higher, near 180 — and the point of a return visit is less the exact number than the enduring cost: a single strike on a school, months on, still defines the war for the families it touched, the kind of civilian toll that shapes both Iran's resolve and the international judgment of the campaign.

stories.title: Israel says 'no interest' in joining, but readies plans
stories.summary: Israel — which with the United States launched the war's opening blow, Operation Epic Fury, in late February — has sent a deliberately mixed message about re-entering the renewed war. Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich said Israel has 'no interest' in joining the US military campaign against Iran, a public signal of restraint, even as CNN reported that Israel is quietly readying plans in case it is drawn back in. Israel has stayed off the battlefield since the fighting reignited on 8 July, leaving the current phase a US-Iran duel, but has warned it will respond 'with full force' if Iranian weapons strike its territory — a stance the newly signed US-Israel military-cooperation expansion in the House bill only deepens.
stories.explainer: Israel rejoining is the fastest way to widen the war, because it doubles the fronts Iran must fight and risks reactivating Hezbollah and other 'Axis of Resistance' militias. Smotrich's 'no interest' and CNN's 'readying plans' are not contradictory: they describe a government that wants to stay out but is hedging against a single errant Iranian missile that could trip its 'full force' tripwire.

notes: Morning refresh of 2026-07-24 (previous cut generated 2026-07-23T22:07Z). The manual RSS refresh via scripts/fetch-news.mjs was again blocked by the environment's egress policy (403 on all news hosts), so this draws on the workflow-committed feed (data/news.json, generated 2026-07-24T07:42Z; freshest item 06:48Z). New since the previous briefing: President Trump vowed to fund the war's 'damages' from frozen Iranian assets the US 'controls,' which Tehran's foreign ministry called 'incendiary' and foreign minister Abbas Araghchi answered that 'mindless aggression' won't help a deal; CENTCOM completed a thirteenth consecutive night of strikes and Iran's Revolutionary Guard said it stopped three oil tankers in the Strait of Hormuz; the IRGC branded UK bases used by US bombers 'legitimate targets,' naming a site in south-west England, and Britain said it is 'ready to defend itself'; the US deployed a B-1 bomber toward the region as the 'massive attack' decision still loomed; the House passed its war-powers resolution 214-208 (four Republicans crossing) while Senate Republicans voted to let Trump keep waging the war, leaving no binding brake; the NYT reported a Pentagon website appears to show a lower US death toll than tallies elsewhere; and the WSJ reported Trump is 'losing patience' over a war with no clear end. Featured stories are ranked by systemic significance, scale, breadth, novelty and continuity; overview, keyPoints and stories describe the same events at different altitudes. Contested claims — the threatened 'massive attack' and its likely target, the B-1 deployment, the frozen-assets 'damages' proposal, the war's $37.5bn cost and ~$88bn/$95bn/$1.15tn funding figures, casualty and injury figures (17–18 dead, ~100 wounded, the ~120 Minab-school toll), the '70%' missile-retention estimate, oil and pump prices, insurance multiples, the depth and nature of 'Pickaxe Mountain,' Iran's claim to have stopped three tankers in Hormuz, the IRGC's UK-base target claim, Jordan's interception count, and Iranian- or Houthi-announced actions — are attributed to their sources.
